skill.name: review
skill.description: Review the changes since a fixed point (commit, branch, tag, or merge-base) along two axes — Standards (does the code follow this repo's documented coding standards?) and Spec (does the code match what the originating issue/PRD asked for?). Runs both reviews in parallel sub-agents and reports them side by side. Use when the user wants to review a branch, a PR, work-in-progress changes, or asks to "review since X".
skill.summary: Reviews branch changes against documented standards and the originating spec in separate report sections.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many Markdown backticks as Ruby or shell execution and one weak-crypto match in frontmatter; these are false positives. The skill does instruct agents to run Git diff and log commands with a user-supplied fixed point, so publication is acceptable with a warning about validating or safely passing Git revisions.

medium_findings.title: User-Supplied Git Revision Passed Into Command Instructions
medium_findings.description: The skill tells the agent to accept a user-provided fixed point and use it in git diff and git log commands. This is legitimate for a review skill, but unsafe shell interpolation or unvalidated revision names could allow option injection or command injection in careless implementations.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Git command instructions are explicit and use user-selected input. The file is Markdown guidance rather than executable code, so the risk depends on how the agent executes the command.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Most external-command detections are inline Markdown code formatting for examples, paths, headings, and file names. No Ruby backtick execution syntax or executable Ruby code is present in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown text containing inline code spans, not executable source code. The context is documentation for an agent workflow.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detection Is a Keyword False Positive
low_findings.description: The high weak-cryptography alert points to the frontmatter description line. No cryptographic algorithm, key handling, hash generation, or encryption behavior appears there.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is YAML frontmatter prose describing the skill. The likely match is a substring such as description, not an actual DES or weak-crypto use.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Detection Is Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The cited line describes acceptable Git revision inputs for review scope selection. I did not find evidence of host reconnaissance, secret discovery, network probing, or environment enumeration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line concerns commit, branch, tag, and merge-base selection. It does not instruct the agent to inspect system state outside the repository review task.

dangerous_patterns.title: Git Commands Built From User-Selected Review Base
dangerous_patterns.description: The pattern git diff <fixed-point>...HEAD and git log <fixed-point>..HEAD --oneline uses a user-selected value. Agents should pass this value as a safe argument and reject shell metacharacters or unexpected option-like input.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command pattern is directly documented and depends on user input. It is not malicious, but it is the main place where unsafe execution practices could create risk.

content.user_title: Review Changes Against Standards and Spec
content.value_statement: Code changes can pass tests while missing project rules or requested behavior. This skill compares a diff against standards and the source spec.
actual_capabilities: Compares HEAD against a specified commit, branch, tag, or merge base.
Reviews changes against documented repository coding standards.
Reviews changes against an issue, PRD, spec file, or supplied requirement source.
Runs standards and spec checks in separate sub-agent contexts.
Aggregates both reports under separate Standards and Spec sections.
Reports missing requirements, scope creep, and convention violations.
limitations: Requires a fixed review point before it can start.
Needs accessible standards documents to perform the standards review well.
Needs an issue, PRD, or spec for the spec review axis.
Does not replace automated tests, linters, or type checkers.

use_cases.title: Review a feature branch before a pull request
use_cases.description: Compare branch changes with main and catch missing requirements or project standard violations before requesting review.

use_cases.title: Audit implementation against a product spec
use_cases.description: Check whether delivered code matches the requested behavior and identify scope creep or partial work.

use_cases.title: Validate contributor changes against repo rules
use_cases.description: Use documented standards to review unfamiliar contributions without mixing style findings with spec findings.

prompt_templates.title: Review Current Branch
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review against the main branch.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this branch against main. Use the repository standards and any linked issue you can find.

prompt_templates.title: Review Since Commit
prompt_templates.scenario: Focused review from a known commit.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review since commit abc123. Separate standards findings from spec findings and cite the source for each finding.

prompt_templates.title: Review Against Supplied Spec
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the changes since main against docs/specs/payment-flow.md. Report missing requirements, scope creep, and standard violations separately.

prompt_templates.title: Review Release Candidate
prompt_templates.prompt: Review HEAD against release-base. Use documented standards, issue references in commits, and any matching PRD under docs. Keep Standards and Spec independent.

output_examples.input: Review this branch against main with the linked issue as the spec.
output_examples.output: Standards: one finding about a documented naming rule. Spec: two findings about a missing error state and unrequested behavior.
Standards: no findings. Spec: one partial requirement where the invite expiry is implemented but the resend behavior is missing.

output_examples.input: Review the work-in-progress changes against HEAD~5.
output_examples.output: Standards: two judgment calls tied to repository guidance. Spec: skipped because no issue, PRD, or supplied spec was available.

best_practices: Provide the exact branch, commit, tag, or merge base to review from.
Point to the issue, PRD, or spec when commit messages do not reference it.
Run tests and linters separately before using this skill for review judgment.
anti_patterns: Using it without a fixed review point.
Treating spec findings as style findings, or style findings as spec findings.
Expecting it to replace test execution, security scanning, or compiler checks.

faq.question: What does this skill review?
faq.answer: It reviews a Git diff against repository standards and the originating issue, PRD, or spec.

faq.question: Does it run tests?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on review judgment and asks agents to avoid duplicating checks already covered by tooling.

faq.question: What fixed point should I provide?
faq.answer: Provide a commit, branch, tag, or merge base such as main, HEAD~5, or a release branch.

faq.question: What happens if there is no spec?
faq.answer: The spec review is skipped and the final report notes that no spec was available.

faq.question: Why are Standards and Spec separate?
faq.answer: A change can follow project rules while implementing the wrong behavior, or meet the spec while breaking conventions.

faq.question: Can it review pull requests?
faq.answer: Yes. Use the pull request branch and provide the base branch or commit as the fixed point.
